title: Question: How to visualize planned vs. actual progress in project management
excerpt:
Description: 
I would like to visualize planned vs. actual progress within the project management features in Monday.com. Ideally, I would like to create combo charts, such as a bar chart showing actual progress combined…

posts.author: monomono (Moe Nomura)
posts.cookedHtml:
Description:
I would like to visualize planned vs. actual progress within the project management features in Monday.com. Ideally, I would like to create combo charts, such as a bar chart showing actual progress combined with a line chart showing planned progress. If there is already a way to do this within Monday.com, I would really appreciate guidance on how to set it up.

If it is not currently possible, this is something that would be extremely helpful for project tracking. Being able to compare planned vs. actual performance in one view helps teams understand trends, identify delays, and make better decisions.

Key points I am trying to solve:

  • Display actual vs. planned progress together on a single chart

  • Use combination charts (e.g., bar + line)

  • Compare values by days, weeks, months, or milestones

  • Highlight the variance between plan and actual clearly

  • Use secondary axes when the scales differ

If there is an existing method or workaround inside Monday.com, please let me know. If not, I believe this capability would greatly improve project tracking and communication across teams.

posts.author: YOGESHP (Yogesh Pokale)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @monomono

you can achieve most of what you’re looking for by:

  • Creating separate Line (Planned) and Bar (Actual) widgets on the same Dashboard for side-by-side comparison

  • Using a Formula column to calculate variance (Actual - Planned) and visualizing that in a chart

  • Grouping by date/week/month to compare trends over time

If you need a true combo chart with secondary axes, you can use the monday.com API together with Power Query to pull your data into Power BI, where you can build fully customized reports (combo charts, dual axes, variance visuals, etc.).

I’m happy to share the query we use if that would help.

posts.author: Iryna_Belaya (Iryna Belaya)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @monomono ,

The Board Email Reports app can help in this part:

  • Compare values by days, weeks, months, or milestones
  • Highlight the variance between plan and actual clearly

The app provides custom reports where you can set up a custom trigger for the report generation, what columns from a board to include to it, a period to report on, and an email body.

For example, let’s take the Numeric changes report to track budget changes and financial adjustments for better control and planning.

In the automation rule, you set up the number column to report on, the reporting period, and the recipient (no monday account needed).

As a result, you get the following report showing how the metric has changed over the specified period.

image1272×370 63.6 KB

The report is in a reusable .xls format, so you can build charts and graphs from this file having these 2 columns From (the beginning of the period) and To (the end of the period).

Having the option to add columns that you need in the report, you can add the Plan column to the report and then to the chart.

You can install and test the app.

P.S. Just in case, you can combine it with Smart Spreadsheetto build charts in the dashboard section on monday from any local xls files.

posts.author: monomono (Moe Nomura)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @YOGESHP @Iryna_Belaya,

Thank you both very much for your detailed and helpful responses. I really appreciate you taking the time to share concrete approaches and workarounds.

Unfortunately, in our environment, both API access and app installations are restricted by our Enterprise account administrator, most likely our CIO. Because of these administrative limitations, we cannot use the API, Power Query, or install additional apps such as Board Email Reports, even though the solutions you suggested would otherwise be very effective.

As a short‑term workaround, we plan to export the data to Excel and process it there to build the visualizations we need. However, from a user perspective, it would be much more convenient and efficient if this entire workflow could be completed consistently within Monday.com.

If the native chart functionality were expanded in the future—for example, to support combo charts (bar + line), more flexible X‑ and Y‑axis selections, clearer variance visualization (planned vs. actual), and secondary axes when needed—it would make planned vs. actual progress tracking possible without relying on external tools. This would likely be helpful for many users working in similar Enterprise environments.

Thanks again for your suggestions and insights—they were very helpful in clarifying what would be possible in a less restricted setup.

posts.author: adinap (Adina Pierce)
posts.cookedHtml: Hello Yogesh, I am interested in your query for pulling in monday data to office via powerautomate.

posts.author: cdalbey (Chelsea Dalbey)
posts.cookedHtml: Moe you can make a Monday vibe with this data and visualize there!

posts.author: YOGESHP (Yogesh Pokale)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @adinap

We use this Power Query in Power BI and then use Power Automate to refresh the dataset.

let
// GraphQL query with placeholders
queryText =
“{”“query”“:”“query getItems($cursor: String) { boards(ids: BOARD_ID) { items_page(limit: 500, cursor: $cursor) { items { id name column_values(ids: [““COLUMN_ID_1””, ““COLUMN_ID_2””, ““COLUMN_ID_3””, ““COLUMN_ID_4””, ““COLUMN_ID_5””]) { id text } } cursor } } }”“}”,

// API request
Source =
Web.Contents(
“https://api.monday.com/v2”,
[
Headers = [
#“Content-Type” = “application/json”,
#“Authorization” = “Bearer YOUR_API_KEY”
],
Content = Text.ToBinary(queryText)
]
),

// Convert response to JSON
JsonResponse = Json.Document(Source),

// Navigate response
boards = JsonResponse[data][boards],
items_page = boards{0}[items_page],
items = items_page[items],

// Convert to table
ItemsTable =
Table.FromList(
items,
Splitter.SplitByNothing(),
{“Column1”}
),

// Expand item data
ExpandedItems =
Table.ExpandRecordColumn(
ItemsTable,
“Column1”,
{“id”, “name”, “column_values”}
),

// Expand column values list
ExpandedColumnValues =
Table.ExpandListColumn(
ExpandedItems,
“column_values”
),

// Expand column values details
FinalTable =
Table.ExpandRecordColumn(
ExpandedColumnValues,
“column_values”,
{“id”, “text”},
{“column_id”, “column_value”}
)

in
FinalTable
